Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige Utagawa|ja|歌川広重|12=draft}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]]

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=draft|12=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Translated pages]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|12=draft|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige Utagawa|ja|歌川広重|12=draft|12=draft}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=draft|12=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Translated pages]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]]

Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Translated pages]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]]

revision.comment: 102 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区) up to rev 101769336
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年7月}} {{神社 |名称=住吉神社 |画像=[[ファイル:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} |所在地=[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]一丁目1-14{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 40 | 緯度秒 = 4.7 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 46 | 経度秒 = 59.9 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |地図=Tokyo city |祭神=[[住吉三神]]、息長足姫命、東照御親命 |創建=[[正保]]3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]） |社格=旧[[郷社]] |札所等=[[下町八福神]] |例祭=例大祭（[[8月6日]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}}） |本殿= }} [[ファイル:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|[[歌川広重]]『[[名所江戸百景]]』の内「佃しま住吉乃祭（佃島住吉の祭）」
[[幕末]]期の、当時はまだ行われていた[[海中渡御]]の様子。手前の幟は「住吉大明神」と読める。]] [[ファイル:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|住吉神社の八角神輿]] [[ファイル:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|陶製の扁額に見える、[[有栖川宮幟仁親王]]の筆になる文字]] '''住吉神社'''（すみよしじんじゃ）は、[[日本]]の[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]にある[[神社]]である。旧[[社格]]は[[郷社]][https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ 住吉神社]（公式ウェブサイト）。 == 祭神 == *[[祭神]]は次の5柱。 ; 主祭神 * '''[[住吉三神|底筒男命]]'''（そこつつのおのみこと） * '''[[住吉三神|中筒男命]]'''（なかつつのおのみこと） * '''[[住吉三神|表筒男命]]'''（うわつつのおのみこと） 以上の3柱は「[[住吉三神]]」と総称される。 * '''[[神功皇后|息長足姫命]]'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと） * '''[[徳川家康|東照御親命]]'''（おずまてるみおやのみこと） == 由緒 == : ※[[新暦]]導入以前（[[1872年]]以前）の日付は[[和暦]]による[[旧暦]]。丸括弧内は[[西暦]]で、[[1581年]]以前は[[ユリウス暦]]、[[1582年]]以降は[[グレゴリオ暦]]。 [[天正]]年間（[[1573年|1573]]- [[1592年]]）、[[徳川家康]]が[[上洛]]し、[[摂津国]][[西成郡]]佃村（現・[[大阪市]][[西淀川区]][[佃 (大阪市)|佃]]）にある住吉神社（改称の経緯：[[田蓑神社]]→田蓑姫神社→住吉神社→田蓑神社）に参詣した際、佃村および近隣の大和田村（現在の西淀川区大和田付近）の[[漁師|漁民]]が[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]に[[渡し船]]を出して家康一行を運び、[[白魚]]などを献上した。これを機縁として、以後、両村の漁民は家康から[[西国]]海上[[隠密]]の用を受けたり、[[大坂の陣]]の際には軍船や魚の調達をするなどした。また、家康は両村の漁民に対し、恩賞として全国での[[漁業権#江戸時代|漁業権]]を与えた。 天正18年[[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]（[[1590年]][[8月30日]]）、[[徳川家康]]が[[関東]]下降の際、先述の佃村および大和田村の漁夫33人と[[神職|神主]]・平岡権大夫好次が江戸に移り、[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）には江戸鉄砲洲向かいにある百[[間]]（約180[[メートル|m]]）四方の[[干潟]]を[[江戸幕府|幕府]]から下賜された漁夫らがこれを埋め立てて築島し、永住することになった。この島を故郷の摂津国佃村にちなんで「[[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]」（島は「佃嶋」、村は「佃村」）と命名し、正保3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]）には、息長足姫命（神功皇后）と東照御親命（徳川家康の[[霊魂|霊]]）の分霊を奉遷し、摂津国佃の住吉社（現・田蓑神社）の[[分霊]]（[[住吉三神]]）とともに祀るべく、住吉神社が創建された。 == 概要 == [[元禄]]7年（[[1694年]]）には、佃嶋（現・佃一丁目）に居住する男子からなる[[講]]組織「佃嶋氏子中」が河上正吉らによって結成され、その後、幾多の火災風災に見舞われながらも、[[氏子]]信者の結束によって[[近代#日本|近現代]]に及ぶ。 [[明治]]時代には、[[月島]]と新佃島、[[昭和]]時代には[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]と[[豊海町 (東京都中央区)|豊海]]が埋め立てられ、また、[[加役方人足寄場|人足寄場]]や[[監獄]]として利用されていた石川島（別称：森島、鎧島）が、[[明治]]29年（[[1896年]]）5月に監獄移転に伴って工業・居住の地となり、これらを含めた全島が氏子中に編入されていった。 [[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）には、講組織「佃嶋氏子中」が「[[佃住吉講]]」と改称し、各町会・連合睦会と協力して、3年に一度の例祭（神幸祭）を執り行うようになる。[[獅子頭]]宮出し、宮[[神輿]]宮出し、古から行われていた神輿の[[海中渡御]]と[[船渡御]]は昭和37年（[[1962年]]）に廃止されたが、[[平成]]2年（[[1990年]]）には28年ぶりに船渡御が復活し、現在でも例祭中最も重要な行事の一つとされている。特に、住吉神社が所有する[[神輿]]は関東では珍しい八角形のものであり、「八角神輿」「八角」などと呼ばれている。また、古来、この祭りの際には高さ20mに及ぶ6本の大[[幟]]が佃島に立つ[[#佃住吉講website|佃住吉講（公式ウェブサイト）]]が、[[江戸城]]からも見えるとまでいわれたその大きさで見る者を圧巻させてきた。 [[御旅所]]が[[勝どき]]四丁目（最初の所在地は勝どき二丁目）に、[[分社]]が[[江東区]]牡丹町（旧・佃町）と[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]にある。 境内の[[鳥居]]に格式高く飾られている[[陶磁器|陶製]]の扁額に見える文字は[[有栖川宮幟仁親王]]の筆による{{sfn|中央区教育委員会|2007|p=6}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html |title=中央区民文化財4　陶製住吉神社扁額（とうせいすみよしじんじゃへんがく） |publisher =中央区 |accessdate=2023-03-06}}。 == 年表 == * [[天正]]年間（[[1573年|1573]] - [[1592年]]）：[[徳川家康]]が[[上洛]]し、[[摂津国]][[西成郡]]佃村（現・[[大阪市]][[西淀川区]][[佃 (大阪市)|佃]]）にある住吉神社（旧・[[田蓑神社]]、現・田蓑神社）に参詣した際、佃村および近隣の大和田村（現在の西淀川区大和田付近）の漁民が[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]に[[渡し船]]を出して家康一行を運ぶなどし、以後、篤い縁を養う。 * 天正18年（[[1590年]]） [[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]（[[8月30日]]）：徳川家康が[[関東]]に下降（[[江戸城]]に公式入城する）し、佃村と大和田村の漁夫33人と住吉神社の[[神職|神主]]・平岡権大夫好次も[[江戸]]に入る。 * [[寛永]]年間（[[1624年|1624]] - [[1645年]]）：江戸鉄砲洲向かいにある百間四方の[[干潟]]を[[江戸幕府|幕府]]から下賜された漁夫らがこれを埋め立てて築島し始める。 *[[寛永]]15年([[1638年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）：築島が成り、「佃」（島は「佃嶋」、村は「佃村」）と名付けて定住。 * 正保3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]）：佃嶋の築島者たちが、息長足姫命（[[神功皇后]]）と東照御親命（徳川家康の霊）の分霊を奉遷し、住吉三神とともに祀るべく、住吉神社を創建する。 * [[元禄]]7年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]（[[1694年]][[9月24日]]）：河上正吉らが[[講]]組織「佃嶋氏子中」を結成。 * [[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[天保]]5年([[1834年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[天保]]9年([[1838年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[安政]]3年（[[1856年]]） - 5年（[[1858年]]）：この頃、[[歌川広重]]が[[名所絵]]揃物『[[名所江戸百景]]』の一図「佃しま住吉乃祭」として、住吉神社例大祭の様子を描く。 * [[安政]]5年([[1858年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[慶応]]2年([[1866年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）：佃嶋氏子中が名称を「[[佃住吉講]]」に変更。 * 昭和37年（[[1962年]]）：[[東京湾]]の[[海洋汚染|汚染]]が進むなか、神輿の[[海中渡御]]と[[船渡御]]が廃止される。 * [[平成]]2年（[[1990年]]）：東京湾の汚染状況が改善されて久しく、28年ぶりに船渡御の復活が成る。 * 平成17年（[[2005年]]）[[8月5日|8月5]] - [[8月8日|8日]]：平成17年例大祭を「住吉神社鎮座360年記念例大祭[[#H17例大祭, 勝どき|住吉神社例大祭 平成17年（勝どき西町会）]]」とし、東京湾上での海上祭と合わせて執行。 * 平成21年（[[2009年]]）[[8月2日]]：50年ぶりに[[千貫神輿]]氏子地域、月島渡り奉祝記念巡行が復活。 == 摂社 == 摂社は計6社中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会） *竜神社 - 文政5年（西暦1822年）奉祀 *疫神社 - 嘉永3年（西暦1850年）奉祀 *疱瘡神社 - 嘉永3年（西暦1850年）奉祀 *船魂神社 *入船神社 *稲荷社 File:竜神社.jpg|竜神社（南から見る） File:疫神社、疱瘡神社.jpg|疫神社、疱瘡神社(北から見る、写真の左が疫神社、右が疱瘡神社) File:船魂神社.jpg|船魂神社（北から見る） File:入船稲荷神社.jpg|入船稲荷神社（西から東側を見る） == 文化財 == === 中央区指定有形文化財 === *水盤舎{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}}{{sfn |中央区の区民文化財（中央区教育委員会）}} *陶製住吉神社扁額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}}{{sfn |中央区の区民文化財（中央区教育委員会）}} *板絵着色蘭陵王図額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *木板金地着色蘆鷺図額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *住吉神社文書[http://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/kusei/syokai/tyuobunkazai/sumiyoshijinjyamonjyo.html 住吉神社文書]（東京都中央区ホームページ「中央区文化財」） === その他 === *植樹の碑　1基{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *五世川柳句碑　1基{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} == 氏子地域 == * [[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]一丁目～三丁目 * 中央区[[月島]]一丁目～四丁目 * 中央区[[勝どき]]一丁目～六丁目 * 中央区[[豊海町 (東京都中央区)|豊海町]] * 中央区[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]一丁目～五丁目 == 関連する創作作品 == * [[丸谷才一]]の短編小説『鈍感な青年』には、3年に一度の例祭以外の年の「かげまつり」の描写が印象深く描かれている。 == 脚注・出典 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=[[大阪都市協会]]編 |title=西淀川区史 |date=1996 |publisher=西淀川区制七十周年記念事業実行委員会 |isbn= |ref=OsakaToshiKyokai (1996)}} *{{Cite book|和書|author= |editor=[[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]]教育委員会 |title=中央区民文化財ガイド 月島編 |date=2007-03 |publisher=中央区教育委員会 |page= |isbn= |ref={{SfnRef|中央区教育委員会|2007}}}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/chuou/3140/|title=住吉神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-20|ref=harv}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} * [[佃 (東京都中央区)]] * [[佃住吉講]] * [[佃 (大阪市)]] * [[田蓑神社]] == {{Anchors|佃住吉講website}}{{Cite web|和書|title=佃住吉講と佃祭り（佃島住吉の祭り）|url=http://www.tsukuda.chuo.tokyo.jp/ |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=佃住吉講 |date= |accessdate=2011-07-02}} * {{Anchors|勝どき西町会website}}{{Cite revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] to [[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年7月}} {{神社 |名称=住吉神社 |画像=[[ファイル:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} |所在地=[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]一丁目1-14{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 40 | 緯度秒 = 4.7 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 46 | 経度秒 = 59.9 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |地図=Tokyo city |祭神=[[住吉三神]]、息長足姫命、東照御親命 |創建=[[正保]]3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]） |社格=旧[[郷社]] |札所等=[[下町八福神]] |例祭=例大祭（[[8月6日]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}}） |本殿= }} [[ファイル:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|[[歌川広重]]『[[名所江戸百景]]』の内「佃しま住吉乃祭（佃島住吉の祭）」
[[幕末]]期の、当時はまだ行われていた[[海中渡御]]の様子。手前の幟は「住吉大明神」と読める。]] [[ファイル:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|住吉神社の八角神輿]] [[ファイル:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|陶製の扁額に見える、[[有栖川宮幟仁親王]]の筆になる文字]] '''住吉神社'''（すみよしじんじゃ）は、[[日本]]の[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]にある[[神社]]である。旧[[社格]]は[[郷社]][https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ 住吉神社]（公式ウェブサイト）。 == 祭神 == *[[祭神]]は次の5柱。 ; 主祭神 * '''[[住吉三神|底筒男命]]'''（そこつつのおのみこと） * '''[[住吉三神|中筒男命]]'''（なかつつのおのみこと） * '''[[住吉三神|表筒男命]]'''（うわつつのおのみこと） 以上の3柱は「[[住吉三神]]」と総称される。 * '''[[神功皇后|息長足姫命]]'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと） * '''[[徳川家康|東照御親命]]'''（おずまてるみおやのみこと） == 由緒 == : ※[[新暦]]導入以前（[[1872年]]以前）の日付は[[和暦]]による[[旧暦]]。丸括弧内は[[西暦]]で、[[1581年]]以前は[[ユリウス暦]]、[[1582年]]以降は[[グレゴリオ暦]]。 [[天正]]年間（[[1573年|1573]]- [[1592年]]）、[[徳川家康]]が[[上洛]]し、[[摂津国]][[西成郡]]佃村（現・[[大阪市]][[西淀川区]][[佃 (大阪市)|佃]]）にある住吉神社（改称の経緯：[[田蓑神社]]→田蓑姫神社→住吉神社→田蓑神社）に参詣した際、佃村および近隣の大和田村（現在の西淀川区大和田付近）の[[漁師|漁民]]が[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]に[[渡し船]]を出して家康一行を運び、[[白魚]]などを献上した。これを機縁として、以後、両村の漁民は家康から[[西国]]海上[[隠密]]の用を受けたり、[[大坂の陣]]の際には軍船や魚の調達をするなどした。また、家康は両村の漁民に対し、恩賞として全国での[[漁業権#江戸時代|漁業権]]を与えた。 天正18年[[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]（[[1590年]][[8月30日]]）、[[徳川家康]]が[[関東]]下降の際、先述の佃村および大和田村の漁夫33人と[[神職|神主]]・平岡権大夫好次が江戸に移り、[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）には江戸鉄砲洲向かいにある百[[間]]（約180[[メートル|m]]）四方の[[干潟]]を[[江戸幕府|幕府]]から下賜された漁夫らがこれを埋め立てて築島し、永住することになった。この島を故郷の摂津国佃村にちなんで「[[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]」（島は「佃嶋」、村は「佃村」）と命名し、正保3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]）には、息長足姫命（神功皇后）と東照御親命（徳川家康の[[霊魂|霊]]）の分霊を奉遷し、摂津国佃の住吉社（現・田蓑神社）の[[分霊]]（[[住吉三神]]）とともに祀るべく、住吉神社が創建された。 == 概要 == [[元禄]]7年（[[1694年]]）には、佃嶋（現・佃一丁目）に居住する男子からなる[[講]]組織「佃嶋氏子中」が河上正吉らによって結成され、その後、幾多の火災風災に見舞われながらも、[[氏子]]信者の結束によって[[近代#日本|近現代]]に及ぶ。 [[明治]]時代には、[[月島]]と新佃島、[[昭和]]時代には[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]と[[豊海町 (東京都中央区)|豊海]]が埋め立てられ、また、[[加役方人足寄場|人足寄場]]や[[監獄]]として利用されていた石川島（別称：森島、鎧島）が、[[明治]]29年（[[1896年]]）5月に監獄移転に伴って工業・居住の地となり、これらを含めた全島が氏子中に編入されていった。 [[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）には、講組織「佃嶋氏子中」が「[[佃住吉講]]」と改称し、各町会・連合睦会と協力して、3年に一度の例祭（神幸祭）を執り行うようになる。[[獅子頭]]宮出し、宮[[神輿]]宮出し、古から行われていた神輿の[[海中渡御]]と[[船渡御]]は昭和37年（[[1962年]]）に廃止されたが、[[平成]]2年（[[1990年]]）には28年ぶりに船渡御が復活し、現在でも例祭中最も重要な行事の一つとされている。特に、住吉神社が所有する[[神輿]]は関東では珍しい八角形のものであり、「八角神輿」「八角」などと呼ばれている。また、古来、この祭りの際には高さ20mに及ぶ6本の大[[幟]]が佃島に立つ[[#佃住吉講website|佃住吉講（公式ウェブサイト）]]が、[[江戸城]]からも見えるとまでいわれたその大きさで見る者を圧巻させてきた。 [[御旅所]]が[[勝どき]]四丁目（最初の所在地は勝どき二丁目）に、[[分社]]が[[江東区]]牡丹町（旧・佃町）と[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]にある。 境内の[[鳥居]]に格式高く飾られている[[陶磁器|陶製]]の扁額に見える文字は[[有栖川宮幟仁親王]]の筆による{{sfn|中央区教育委員会|2007|p=6}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html |title=中央区民文化財4　陶製住吉神社扁額（とうせいすみよしじんじゃへんがく） |publisher =中央区 |accessdate=2023-03-06}}。 == 年表 == * [[天正]]年間（[[1573年|1573]] - [[1592年]]）：[[徳川家康]]が[[上洛]]し、[[摂津国]][[西成郡]]佃村（現・[[大阪市]][[西淀川区]][[佃 (大阪市)|佃]]）にある住吉神社（旧・[[田蓑神社]]、現・田蓑神社）に参詣した際、佃村および近隣の大和田村（現在の西淀川区大和田付近）の漁民が[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]に[[渡し船]]を出して家康一行を運ぶなどし、以後、篤い縁を養う。 * 天正18年（[[1590年]]） [[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]（[[8月30日]]）：徳川家康が[[関東]]に下降（[[江戸城]]に公式入城する）し、佃村と大和田村の漁夫33人と住吉神社の[[神職|神主]]・平岡権大夫好次も[[江戸]]に入る。 * [[寛永]]年間（[[1624年|1624]] - [[1645年]]）：江戸鉄砲洲向かいにある百間四方の[[干潟]]を[[江戸幕府|幕府]]から下賜された漁夫らがこれを埋め立てて築島し始める。 *[[寛永]]15年([[1638年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）：築島が成り、「佃」（島は「佃嶋」、村は「佃村」）と名付けて定住。 * 正保3年[[6月29日 (旧暦)|6月29日]]（[[1646年]][[8月10日]]）：佃嶋の築島者たちが、息長足姫命（[[神功皇后]]）と東照御親命（徳川家康の霊）の分霊を奉遷し、住吉三神とともに祀るべく、住吉神社を創建する。 * [[元禄]]7年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]（[[1694年]][[9月24日]]）：河上正吉らが[[講]]組織「佃嶋氏子中」を結成。 * [[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[天保]]5年([[1834年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[天保]]9年([[1838年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[安政]]3年（[[1856年]]） - 5年（[[1858年]]）：この頃、[[歌川広重]]が[[名所絵]]揃物『[[名所江戸百景]]』の一図「佃しま住吉乃祭」として、住吉神社例大祭の様子を描く。 * [[安政]]5年([[1858年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[慶応]]2年([[1866年]]）:社殿が類焼する。{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} * [[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）：佃嶋氏子中が名称を「[[佃住吉講]]」に変更。 * 昭和37年（[[1962年]]）：[[東京湾]]の[[海洋汚染|汚染]]が進むなか、神輿の[[海中渡御]]と[[船渡御]]が廃止される。 * [[平成]]2年（[[1990年]]）：東京湾の汚染状況が改善されて久しく、28年ぶりに船渡御の復活が成る。 * 平成17年（[[2005年]]）[[8月5日|8月5]] - [[8月8日|8日]]：平成17年例大祭を「住吉神社鎮座360年記念例大祭[[#H17例大祭, 勝どき|住吉神社例大祭 平成17年（勝どき西町会）]]」とし、東京湾上での海上祭と合わせて執行。 * 平成21年（[[2009年]]）[[8月2日]]：50年ぶりに[[千貫神輿]]氏子地域、月島渡り奉祝記念巡行が復活。 == 摂社 == 摂社は計6社中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会） *竜神社 - 文政5年（西暦1822年）奉祀 *疫神社 - 嘉永3年（西暦1850年）奉祀 *疱瘡神社 - 嘉永3年（西暦1850年）奉祀 *船魂神社 *入船神社 *稲荷社 File:竜神社.jpg|竜神社（南から見る） File:疫神社、疱瘡神社.jpg|疫神社、疱瘡神社(北から見る、写真の左が疫神社、右が疱瘡神社) File:船魂神社.jpg|船魂神社（北から見る） File:入船稲荷神社.jpg|入船稲荷神社（西から東側を見る） == 文化財 == === 中央区指定有形文化財 === *水盤舎{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}}{{sfn |中央区の区民文化財（中央区教育委員会）}} *陶製住吉神社扁額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}}{{sfn |中央区の区民文化財（中央区教育委員会）}} *板絵着色蘭陵王図額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *木板金地着色蘆鷺図額{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *住吉神社文書[http://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/kusei/syokai/tyuobunkazai/sumiyoshijinjyamonjyo.html 住吉神社文書]（東京都中央区ホームページ「中央区文化財」） === その他 === *植樹の碑　1基{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} *五世川柳句碑　1基{{sfn |中央区の文化財（四）　有形民俗文化財　-信仰-　（中央区教育委員会）}} == 氏子地域 == * [[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[佃 (東京都中央区)|佃]]一丁目～三丁目 * 中央区[[月島]]一丁目～四丁目 * 中央区[[勝どき]]一丁目～六丁目 * 中央区[[豊海町 (東京都中央区)|豊海町]] * 中央区[[晴海 (東京都中央区)|晴海]]一丁目～五丁目 == 関連する創作作品 == * [[丸谷才一]]の短編小説『鈍感な青年』には、3年に一度の例祭以外の年の「かげまつり」の描写が印象深く描かれている。 == 脚注・出典 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=[[大阪都市協会]]編 |title=西淀川区史 |date=1996 |publisher=西淀川区制七十周年記念事業実行委員会 |isbn= |ref=OsakaToshiKyokai (1996)}} *{{Cite book|和書|author= |editor=[[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]]教育委員会 |title=中央区民文化財ガイド 月島編 |date=2007-03 |publisher=中央区教育委員会 |page= |isbn= |ref={{SfnRef|中央区教育委員会|2007}}}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/chuou/3140/|title=住吉神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-20|ref=harv}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} * [[佃 (東京都中央区)]] * [[佃住吉講]] * [[佃 (大阪市)]] * [[田蓑神社]] == 外部リンク == * {{Anchors|official website}}{{Cite web|和書|title=住吉神社 |url=http://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/index.html |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=住吉神社 |date= |accessdate=2011-07-02}} * {{Anchors|佃住吉講website}}{{Cite web|和書|title=佃住吉講と佃祭り（佃島住吉の祭り）|url=http://www.tsukuda.chuo.tokyo.jp/ |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=佃住吉講 |date= |accessdate=2011-07-02}} * {{Anchors|勝どき西町会website}}{{Cite web|和書|title=勝どき西町会 |url=http://kachidoki.org/ |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=勝どき西町会 |date= |accessdate=2011-07-02}} ** {{Anchors|H17例大祭, 勝どき}}{{Cite web|和書|title=住吉神社例大祭 平成17年 |url=http://kachidoki.org/100/ |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=勝どき西町会 |date= |accessdate=2011-07-02}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:すみよししんしや とうきようとちゆうおうく}} [[Category:東京都中央区の神社]] revision.text:
{{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige Utagawa|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Translated pages]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|ja|名所江戸百景|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige Utagawa|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]]

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]]

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]]

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

revision.text:
{{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}}

Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima.
