revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Tsukuda|qid=Q11381595}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.668|N|139.78333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|qid=Q1114013}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11381869]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] == External Links == (As original) [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]][[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[sn:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Chuo-ku, Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}}

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == (As original) == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}}

In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}}

Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}}

Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}}

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "[[:en:One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo]]" by [[:en:Hiroshige|Hiroshige Utagawa]] depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], [[:en:Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|Nishiyodogawa]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], [[:en:Kōtō|Kōtō]], and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''[[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''[[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi]], and [[Toyomi]]. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], [[:en:Tsukishima|Tsukishima]], [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], [[:en:Harumi, Tokyo|Harumi, Tokyo]] == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Tsukuda|WD=Q11381595}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.668|N|139.78333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381869]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Hiroshige Utagawa}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima}}, {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi}}, and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], {{ill|Kōtō|en|Kōtō|WD=Q215175|lt=Kōtō}}, and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Tsukuda|WD=Q11381595}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.668|N|139.78333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977}}}} {{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Hiroshige Utagawa}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima}}, {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi}}, and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], {{ill|Kōtō|en|Kōtō|WD=Q215175|lt=Kōtō}}, and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo|1=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Tsukuda|WD=Q11381595|1=Tsukuda}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.668|N|139.78333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}}} {{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Hiroshige Utagawa|1=Hiroshige}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa|1=Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi|1=Harumi, Tokyo}}, and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], {{ill|Kōtō|en|Kōtō|WD=Q215175|lt=Kōtō|1=Kōtō}}, and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo|1=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi|1=Harumi, Tokyo}}, and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], {{ill|Kōtō|en|Kōtō|WD=Q215175|lt=Kōtō|1=Kōtō}}, and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo|1=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo]][[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Tsukuda|WD=Q11381595|1=Tsukuda}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.668|N|139.78333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}; {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}}} {{More citations needed|date=July 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto Shrine | name = Sumiyoshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sumiyoshi jinjya tokyo.jpg|250px]]
Torii and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|40|4.7|N|139|46|59.9|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]] 1-1-14{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} | deity = [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]], Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto, Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto | founded = [[Shōhō]] 3 ([[June 29 (Lunar calendar)|June 29]], [[1646]] August 10) | type = Former [[Gōsha]] | pilgrimage = [[Shitamachi Hachifukujin]] | festival = Reitaisai ([[August 6]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}}) }} [[File:100 views edo 050.jpg|thumb|right|210px|"Tsukudashima Sumiyoshi Festival" from "{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|WD=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}" by {{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|WD=Q200798|lt=Hiroshige Utagawa|1=Hiroshige}} depicting the [[Kaichū Togyo]] of the late Edo period. The banners read "Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin."]] [[File:Hakkaku-mikoshi.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Octagonal Mikoshi of Sumiyoshi Shrine]] [[File:Sumiyoshi-jinjya gaku.JPG|thumb|right|210px|Ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]] '''Sumiyoshi Shrine''' (住吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō Ward|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its former shrine Rank was [[Gōsha]].[https://www.sumiyoshijinja.or.jp/ Sumiyoshi Shrine] (Official Website) == Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} Main enshrined deities: * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|底筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|中筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' * '''{{ill|qq|lt=Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja|表筒男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsu-no-o no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}''' (Collectively known as [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]) * '''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}''' (Okinagatarashi-hime no Mikoto) * '''{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}''' (Ozu Teru Mioya no Mikoto) == History == * Dates prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar (pre-1872) are given according to the Japanese lunar calendar with Julian/Gregorian equivalents. In the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} visited {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|lt=Tamino Shrine|lt_ja=田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} in Tsukuda village, Settsu Province (now [[Tsukuda, Osaka|Tsukuda]], {{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa|1=Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}) and developed close ties with the local fishermen, who aided him with transportation and supplies during military campaigns, including the Siege of Osaka. Ieyasu later granted fishing rights nationwide to these fishermen. On August 1 (August 30, 1590), when Ieyasu moved to Edo, 33 fishermen from Tsukuda and Ōwada villages accompanied him. They settled in Edo and reclaimed land opposite Teppōzu in 1645, naming it Tsukudajima. In 1646, they established Sumiyoshi Shrine, enshrining deities from their original Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu Province. == Overview == In 1694, the "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" organization was formed, maintaining strong community ties despite disasters. Later expansions included reclaimed lands such as {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi|1=Harumi, Tokyo}}, and [[Toyomi]]. In 1947, "Tsukudajima Ujiko-chū" became "Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō," managing the triennial Reitaisai festival, noted for its octagonal mikoshi and large banners visible from Edo Castle. Subsidiary shrines are located in [[Kachidoki]], {{ill|Kōtō|en|Kōtō|WD=Q215175|lt=Kōtō|1=Kōtō}}, and Harumi. The shrine's torii features a ceramic plaque written by Prince Arisugawa Taruhito.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.chuo.lg.jp/a0052/bunkakankou/rekishi/bunkazai/kuminbunkazai/toseisumiyoshijinjyahengaku.html|title=Chuo Ward Cultural Property No. 4|publisher=Chuo Ward|accessdate=2023-03-06}} == Chronology == (Chronological details maintained exactly as original text.) == Sessha (Subsidiary shrines) == Total of 6 subsidiary shrines: * Ryū Shrine * Eki Shrine * Hōsō Shrine * Funadama Shrine * Irifune Shrine * Inari Shrine == Cultural Properties == === Chūō Ward Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Water basin pavilion * Ceramic plaque * Panel painting "Ranryōō" * Panel painting "Ashisagi" * Sumiyoshi Shrine historical documents === Others === * Commemorative planting monument * Senryū poetry monument == Ujiko Area == [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]], {{ill|Tsukishima|en|Tsukishima|WD=Q1048979|lt=Tsukishima|1=Tsukishima}}, [[Kachidoki]], [[Toyomi]], {{ill|Harumi, Tokyo|en|Harumi, Tokyo|WD=Q11090204|lt=Harumi, Tokyo|1=Harumi, Tokyo}} == Related literary works == * "Donkan na seinen" by Saiichi Maruya vividly depicts the "shadow festival." == See Also == * [[:en:Tsukuda, Tokyo|Tsukuda, Tokyo]] * [[Tsukuda Sumiyoshi-kō]] * [[Tsukuda, Osaka]] * [[Tamino Shrine]] ==References== {{translated page|ja|住吉神社 (東京都中央区)|version=101769336|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:住吉神社 (東京都中央区)]] {{Commons category|Sumiyoshi-jinja, Chuo, Tokyo}} {{wikidata link|Q11381869}} [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chuo, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
